transcript.text: Hello, here's a request video. What do I think about Noam Chomsky? Requested by Luca Serbic from Serbia
Um, yeah, I've always known who Noam Chomsky was. Uh, he's, uh, born in, like, I just listened to the, uh, Wikipedia article about him. He was born in, uh, December 7th, 28th, so he's, like, 84 now.
Um, he was younger than my dad. But, um, he, um, I, I always knew who he was because in college, I, I, some, one of his books, or I guess a couple of his books were assigned to us to read.
I took a linguistics class, and maybe that was a class where I was supposed to read one of his books. Um, he's the father of linguistics.

Um, I guess he was, he's responsible for the, um, like, an evolution in, uh, like, I guess psychology, where up until, um, I guess the 60s, when he, when he wrote his books, invented, uh, linguistics, uh, psychology was all, like, behavioral psychology learned from Freud and Jung.

Um, and then after him, it was just, like, they looked at it more scientifically in terms of just, like, uh, um, kind of similar to, like, neuroscience, like, uh, cause and effect, like, uh, actually studying, like, what you say and, like, the meanings of words and all that kind of stuff.

But, but anyways, he's a polymath, though, that's the main thing. He, he, uh, he, I saw a bunch of videos from him on YouTube doing lectures, and he does lectures on all kinds of stuff, and he just knows everything about everything, and, and, um, about, like, politics and, um, history and all that kind of stuff.
But, yeah, I guess he, he's mainly, uh, a linguistics guy in, uh, the university of, or MIT for, like, the last 55 years or something.
Um, and he was saying some interesting stuff in one of his lectures that Helen Keller, she never, uh, she, in the age of, like, 20 months, just over a year and a half old,
She got some, she got spinal meningitis, and it took away her, uh, sight and hearing.

And so she was, you know, she was blind and deaf, but she could still communicate with everybody, you know, and, and, uh, invented, like, sign language and, um, you know, wrote books and all that kind of stuff.
I remember seeing a movie about her when I was a kid, and people were all, like, amazed by it, because they're, like, oh, my God, how does she know so much if she's blind and deaf?
And he said, well, because she lost it at the age of 20 months, you know, she had learned it since then, and people learn, like, little kids learn a lot more than, uh, than we realize.
And she was saying that maybe at the age of 20 months, little kids know every single word in the vocabulary.
They'll learn it all, they just can't use it yet.
Um, and he says that we still don't completely understand, like, how little kids learn words, um, because it's just, to them, it's just confusing.
It's just this, just, uh, confusing, uh, words just, you know, coming at them that they just have to somehow place.
But they do, I think people learn it because, because of a past life memory.
You know, even somebody who's born deaf and blind and deaf, I don't know if they're people like that, but I bet you they know stuff, too, just out of intuition,
Because they're connected to the world, a wider world of random, through their mind.
Um, but, but, uh, yeah, he learned, he listened, he talked a lot of, uh, I listened to a lot of his speeches about, uh, politics.
And, uh, he's very, he's very provocative.
He's extremely, um, liberal, like, anti-oligarchy.
Um, and I'm sure a lot of the, uh, powers that be don't like him at all.
In the, in the, uh, Wikipedia article, they said he was detained, trying to get into Israel for, like, a couple hours.
Uh, and, um, he said it was because the powers that be didn't want him in, but they said they tried to explain and say, oh, no, it was just the border guard's fault.
But, because he's really popular.
He's like a hero.
He's been given honorary degrees to, like, a hundred different colleges around the world and universities.
And, um, people revere him.
He's like, uh, he's like a prophet, like the smartest guy ever, because he knows everything about everything.
He's what you call a smarty pants.
Um, because, yeah, he has a really good knowledge.
I mean, you ask him anything about a policy or anything or a point in history, you know, in the last 200 years, and he's, he knows all about it.
He's kind of boring the way he talks, though.
He talks in a monotone voice like this, and he's probably always talked like that, but, you know, how old people are.

But, uh, but, yeah, I mean, for how old he is, he's extremely, I mean, you know, like, supportive of, uh, the, uh, the poor and, uh, anti-establishment, and, uh, which is impressive for somebody that old.
And, um, even more so than I would be, you know, like he says that, you know, America's the biggest, uh, terrorist in the world.
And, um, you know, Republican Party and Democrat Party are the same party.
And Fox News is, uh, isn't real news.
It's just panders to the, uh, to the, uh, you know, the, uh, the big business.
And, uh, Glenn Beck and Rush Limbrow are crazy.
Um, but, uh, I guess his background as he was raised, he's Jewish.
And, you know, and even though he's raised Jewish, he's still, you know, not, doesn't support the, all of the Jewish settlements in, uh, Gaza.
And, um, doesn't even think that Israel had a right to exist in the beginning in the first place.
You know, like you shouldn't start a, a country just because, uh, of a religion, you know, when there was already, uh, you know, occupied by other people who were there or another country.
Uh, I heard, though, that, this is an aside, but I heard that, um, uh, you know, Mark Twain traveled around the world in the 1860s and he went through, uh, Palestine.
He said there was nobody there.
So when the Jews went there, you know, they were basically taking up desert and turned the desert into farming territory and turned the whole area around.
So, and I tend to believe that.
But anyways, yeah, I mean, it's impressive that he'd be anti-Israel even though he's, uh, he's Jewish himself.
He said there was a lot of anti-Semitism when he was growing up and his parents were both immigrants.
And so he was, he grew up, um, kind of, uh, you know, uh, I don't know if he was poor, but he grew up, he worked in the factories.

And he, he, one thing he likes to mention a lot is, uh, how the, how, uh, factory workers don't, um, in those days, in the past, you know, when factories were first came about, they did not like to work in the factories.
They thought it was not fair to them.
And he says that wage or earners is like similar.
It's just like one step up from slavery and, uh, people should get paid just for their work.
And, you know, I think that's a great revolutionary idea that you just pay somebody for their work and, and, uh, you know, don't like have to have to make them like have the job.
Oh, you have to show up at the same time every day and, and get paid by the hour and do, you know, what you tell them to.
I mean, there was a lot of that bad, you know, exploitation going on in the beginning of the industrial revolution, during the industrial revolution.

But, uh, but one thing he said that I found really interesting is that, well, as I'm sure most of us know that there's different viewpoints of the world and the, you know, the, the elite, um, like to preserve their power.
And, uh, they, um, they always had, um, you know, like when your, when your universities were first invented, it was only for the elite.

And, uh, even up until the sixties, like when he was in college, Harvard was, was just, uh, elite institution that was just there to basically get all of the elite, you know, rich people of society acquainted with each other.
And, uh, so that they could carry on those business dealings for the rest of their lives.

And I mean, it's still like that now pretty much, but, uh, but then, then it was, it was, uh, it was just a lot more pronounced, but he says that America is a lot less, um, socially stratified than Europe.
Like in Europe, you would, the rich people would kind of not even bother to talk to the poor.
And, uh, in America, you know, the rich can talk to the poor.
I saw that a lot in Chile when I lived there that, you know, the rich thought we're afraid of the poor.

The thing went to the poor's neighborhood, they get mugged and then the poor didn't like the rich cause they, they wouldn't, they felt like they thought they were too good for him cause they wouldn't talk to him and stuff.
And so he says America is the best country in the world because of that, because also because we have our, we, we take the freedom of speech a lot more seriously than anybody else in the world.
Um, and, um, but, uh, but yeah, nevertheless, we are the biggest, uh, terrorists, he says.

Um, he, um, he says that, uh, uh, uh, oh yeah, one interesting thing though that he said is that, you know, the factory workers when they work, um, I guess maybe the ones that he worked with and, and then the beginnings of, of, of the factory days.
Um, those, um, those factory workers were very interested in educating themselves and reading.

They weren't like stupid, useless eaters, like the elite like to, um, kind of portray them as, you know, like, um, basically to try to tell them that, you know, they're going to tell them how it is and they don't get to have any say because they're not interested anyways.
Which is kind of a, yeah, an idea that I have a lot of lower working classes because I've, you know, offered to share my audio books with people and stuff and they're never interested.

But, um, but he said back in those days, you know, like they were all reading like the, the lower in the heyday of newspapers, I guess, in the mid 1800s, the, uh, the small newspapers run by just small towns and just, you know, small time factory type people were in just took a, had just as much circulation as the overall, as the, uh, the big newspapers.

And, um, and, uh, you know, small business owners like the guys who, you know, the Smith and the, and the carpenter and stuff, you know, shop workers and even factory workers were interested in reading all the time and educating themselves about politics and everything.
And they would have like, you know, their, their underlings or their children read to them while they were working and stuff.
And so, and so he has a lot of, uh, sympathy for the working class and he thinks they're just as smart and capable as the, uh, as the upper class, which, which is something that I agree with.

I think that if you give people the chance and, and, you know, enough spare time in the day, they're not just going to be like, um, they're not just going to be, you know, leeches on society, which is what the dudes on Fox news.
And a lot of those, you know, ultra conservative and like Republican type of, uh, politicians and people try to make them out to be, you know, like we don't want welfare.
Well, let's cut welfare.
Those people, they're just going to, you, you give them food, you give them an inch.
They're going to take a mile.
People are just lazy.
You got to keep them working, keep them, keep them poor.
So they have to work and all this shit.
And he's like, no, if you give people a chance, they're going to try and work.
And that's what I believe.

If you give people, uh, if you give people, you know, pay them enough wages to, so that they don't have to work like 12 hours a day, six days a week, you know, where they would have to work only three day, three hours a day or something like that.
Five days a week, you know, then they're, they're not going to spend the rest of their time just drinking beer and playing video games.
They're going to spend the rest of their time trying to improve the world and improve their education and, um, improve their lives and everything.

And, uh, and, uh, so it's kind of like, is, uh, this, uh, uh, two different ways to view the world, you know, the oligarchical, uh, uh, mindset where there was, these rich people just want to preserve their, their place in, in, uh, society and their wealth.
And then the regular people like, um, or, or the, you know, the populist people, the communist types, um, progressive people who want to try to spread the wealth around as much.
And I think that's the best thought for the world, which is, which is the tribe that I subscribe to.
But, uh, but, um, yeah, he's smart.
I mean, he's kind of difficult to listen to though, because I listened to a lot of his videos and I would lose interest or lose the ability to pay attention to what he's saying.
Cause he's just so monotone, but, but he's smart.
I mean, it's, uh, fascinating to hear him listening.
You know, when people would ask him questions and he could, about anything, I mean, it was huge, huge, wide range of questions and he'd have an answer for it.
Like a big, long answer.
And there are a lot of videos of him talking for like an hour, lectures about, about everything.
I mean, he gives reasons for why, uh, you know, he believes that America is just controlled by big business and we're, we're the, uh, the, um, um, the terrorists of the world.
But, you know, he's very, he's very, uh, provocative.
I mean, I think a lot of times it seems like he's talking just to get a rise out of people.
For example, he calls himself an atheist and he's like, yeah, I mean, but I mean, why would I even call myself an atheist if I don't even understand what I'm supposed to not believe?
Which I don't understand what he means by that.
Maybe he's just trying to be provocative.
But, um, he also calls himself an anarchist, so, but he says that, you know, he's a linguist.
He says that, um, words have different meanings depending on where you are anyways, you know.
Like, here in, uh, America, libertarian means something different than in Europe.
Like, here your libertarian would be more, um, uh, kind of anarchist.
And there it's more, uh, um, I don't know, I can't remember, but, but, uh, you know, that is kind of where the, where the conversations get good and strong.

And, you know, that's where the meat is of the conversation where you're actually debating the meaning of a word and getting, getting to the, having a real conversation about something rather than just saying, you're a set socialism.
You're a socialist.
Oh my God, don't, you know, like black and white freaking out.

But he clearly has no respect for, like, Fox News, and he mentions Glenn Beck and Rush Limbaugh is crazy, and, um, he says that, uh, um, Obama is, you know, we call him like a Democrat, but he, he would, he would say Obama, um, you know, because we've gotten, I guess, a lot more right wing, gone, gone a lot more to the right, you know, business oriented.
Um, he says that we've never really had capitalism.
And capitalism is a nice idea that we try to have, but we never really had it.

We're just, we're mainly living in fascism, which is, which is just controlled, to hunt the country controlled by a small group of just business, um, conglomerates who were tied together, looking out after each other's back, keeping themselves as rich as they can.

Um, you know, but we've gotten even more in that direction in the last 20 years, and he said, you know, like Obama now, because he's so federal, so, you know, so much, taking advantage of so much of the power of the president, getting in the nose of everybody that, you know, back in the 80s, he would be, he would, the way he's acting, he would be considered more like a Republican.
Like a, maybe a little left end of the Republican, um, so, um, but you know, now in these days, I guess he's, he's kind of like a regular Democrat, so I guess everybody's shifted to the right.
But we need to be, uh, so I think he says, yeah, we need to, and the Democratic and Republican Party are just the same party.
And, uh, and the third, and the second party would be, you know, like all those other ones.

And, and I think he does support, like, well, obviously, I don't know, or not, I can't remember his specific name, but I'm sure he supports voting a president who's in a, in a third party or, or a non-party president.
He's really intelligent. I mean, whatever he, most of all of what he says, really, I agree with.

He, um, except for the atheist stuff, but, you know, if you live, if you grew up in an environment where everybody around you is hardcore Christian, I mean, take all that, takes all that too far, then, you know, maybe I'd be knee-jerk too and be like, fuck it all, I don't believe any of it.
But, uh, but, um, yeah, he's interesting. I mean, there's a lot, there's a lot, uh, more videos that I could watch about him that he didn't, uh, that I haven't even seen yet.
His take on education and interesting, he says that, uh, education should be something that, um, causes us to basically think for ourselves and that's not what education really is.
All education is, and, and, uh, unfortunately in the real world is just indoctrinating people and learning by rote and having you repeat after me and that kind of stuff.
Making them go to, you know, like making kids go to the, uh, the lab because in theory it's a cool idea and then you do the experiment that never works.
And he said he didn't even bother to go to the lab because he knew the experiment would never work.
And then he was charged 17 bucks to fix a flask he supposedly broke.
But, uh, he probably would agree with what I believe with education is that it should just be, you know, like test oriented.
And I think that's how it is more in Europe too, where you just go to school to try to do well on the test.
And if you pass the test and you get the degree in America, you just have to please the teacher, please the teacher degree.
It doesn't matter about how much you actually, how much you actually learn of the test.
Um, and, uh, so it's, yeah, too, it's gotten too, uh, you know, hierarchical here.
But, um, he, uh, um, yeah, he says that education is something that we should all, uh, you know, we should all like basically learn to teach ourselves.
And, um, like when kids go and, um, um, you know, go out into the, into the field and pick up spiders and look at them and all that kind of stuff.
That's, that's a real education, you know, student driven education.
And because, because we know our natural tendency as humans is to learn and to study.
And so all you have to do is allow people to do that.
